title: Security in the Pipeline (DevSecOps)
summary: Shift-left security, SAST/DAST/SCA, SBOM, container scanning, supply chain security, and secrets scanning in CI/CD.

sections.content:
## DevSecOps — Shifting Left

DevSecOps integrates security into every phase of the SDLC — not as a gate at the end, but as a continuous activity. The later a vulnerability is found, the more expensive it is to fix.

**Cost of fixing a vulnerability:**
- Design phase: $1
- Development: $10  
- Test/staging: $100
- Production: $10,000+

sections.title: Security in Every Pipeline Stage
sections.content:
graph LR
    Code[Code] -->|SAST| Commit[Commit]
    Commit -->|Secret scan| Build[Build]
    Build -->|SCA dependency scan| Test[Test]
    Test -->|Container scan| Image[Image]
    Image -->|DAST| Staging[Staging]
    Staging -->|Pen test| Prod[Production]
    Prod -->|Runtime protection| Monitor[Monitor]
    style Code fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style Prod fill:#059669,color:#fff

## Security Testing Types

### SAST — Static Application Security Testing
Analyzes source code without running it. Finds injection vulnerabilities, insecure patterns, secrets in code.

```yaml
# GitHub Actions — SAST with Semgrep
- name: SAST Scan
  uses: semgrep/semgrep-action@v1
  with:
    config: >-
      p/owasp-top-ten
      p/java
      p/spring
```

### SCA — Software Composition Analysis
Scans dependencies for known CVEs (Log4Shell, Spring4Shell, etc.)

```yaml
- name: Dependency vulnerability scan
  run: mvn org.owasp:dependency-check-maven:check
  # Fails build if CVSS >= 7.0 (high/critical)
```

### Secret Scanning
Prevents credentials, API keys, and tokens from being committed:

```yaml
- name: Secret scan
  uses: trufflesecurity/trufflehog@main
  with:
    path: ./
    base: ${{ github.event.repository.default_branch }}
    extra_args: --only-verified
```

### Container Image Scanning
```yaml
- name: Trivy container scan
  uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@master
  with:
    image-ref: ${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}:${{ github.sha }}
    exit-code: '1'           # fail build on findings
    severity: 'CRITICAL,HIGH'
    ignore-unfixed: true     # skip unfixed vulns
```

## SBOM — Software Bill of Materials

An SBOM is a machine-readable inventory of all software components and their dependencies — like a food ingredient list for software.

```bash
# Generate SBOM with Syft
syft registry/order-service:1.2.0 -o spdx-json > sbom.json
syft registry/order-service:1.2.0 -o cyclonedx-json >> sbom-cyclonedx.json

# Scan SBOM for vulnerabilities with Grype
grype sbom:sbom.json

# Formats: SPDX (Linux Foundation), CycloneDX (OWASP)
```

SBOM use cases:
- Rapid response to new CVEs (instantly know which services are affected)
- Regulatory compliance (US Executive Order 14028 mandates SBOMs for federal software)
- License compliance (know if any dependency uses GPL, AGPL)

## Supply Chain Security

### Image signing with Cosign
```bash
# Sign container image after build
cosign sign --key cosign.key registry/order-service:sha-abc123

# Verify before deployment (enforce in admission controller)
cosign verify --key cosign.pub registry/order-service:sha-abc123

# Kubernetes admission: only allow signed images (Sigstore/Policy Controller)
```

### Provenance Attestations (SLSA)
SLSA (Supply chain Levels for Software Artifacts) is a framework for verifying software provenance:

```bash
# Attest build provenance — who built this, when, from what source
cosign attest --predicate provenance.json \
  --type slsaprovenance \
  registry/order-service:sha-abc123

# Consumer verifies: this image was built from this exact commit, by this CI
cosign verify-attestation --type slsaprovenance \
  registry/order-service:sha-abc123
```

sections.question: What does a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) enable that traditional security scanning alone cannot?
options: Rapid impact assessment when a new CVE is disclosed — instantly identify all services containing the vulnerable component
sections.explanation: When Log4Shell was disclosed, organizations without SBOMs spent days manually checking which services used Log4j. With SBOMs, you query: 'which of our images contain log4j-core <= 2.14?' and get an answer in seconds. SBOMs also help with license compliance and regulatory requirements for software transparency.
